word_list.word: believe nothing of what you hear, and only half of what you see
word_list.pronunciation: bɪlˈiːv nˈʌθɪŋ ʌv wˌʌt juː hˈɪɹ ænd ˈoʊnli hˈæf ʌv wˌʌt juː sˈiː

senses.definition: used to warn against blindly accepting information as true, advising that one should be skeptical and verify before accepting it

word_list.word: let the wise listen and add to their learning, and let the discerning get guidance
word_list.pronunciation: lˈɛt ðə wˈaɪz lˈɪsən ænd ˈæd tə ðɛɹ lˈɜːnɪŋ ænd lˈɛt ðə dɪsˈɜːnɪŋ ɡɛt ɡˈaɪdəns

senses.definition: used to emphasize the importance of being open-minded, receptive to new knowledge, and seeking guidance to enhance one's wisdom and understanding

word_list.word: a reed before the wind lives on
word_list.pronunciation: ɐ ɹˈiːd bɪfˌoːɹ ðə wˈɪnd lˈaɪvz ˈɑːn

senses.definition: used to highlight the importance of being flexible and adaptable in difficult or changing circumstances, as it can help one to survive and thrive

word_list.word: a wise man changes his mind, a fool never
word_list.pronunciation: ɐ wˈaɪz mˈæn tʃˈeɪndʒᵻz hɪz mˈaɪnd ɐ fˈuːl nˈɛvɚ

senses.definition: used to imply that a wise person is willing to admit their mistakes and change their opinion, while a foolish person is too stubborn or prideful to do so
used to suggest that people should be open and accepting of others despite their differences

word_list.word: if all you have is a hammer, everything looks like a nail
word_list.pronunciation: ɪf ˈɔːl juː hæv ɪz ɐ hˈæmɚ ˈɛvɹɪθˌɪŋ lˈʊks lˈaɪk ɐ nˈeɪl

senses.definition: used to convey that if one's only tool is one narrow skill or way of thinking, one will tend to try to use that single approach for everything, even when it is not the most appropriate solution

word_list.word: you cannot teach an old dog new tricks
word_list.pronunciation: juː kænˈɑːt tˈiːtʃ ɐn ˈoʊld dˈɑːɡ nˈuː tɹˈɪks

senses.definition: used to imply that older people are less adaptable to change and new ideas than younger people, as it is difficult or impossible to change someone's old behaviors or habits

word_list.word: a closed mind is like a closed book (, just a block of wood|)
word_list.pronunciation: ɐ klˈoʊzd mˈaɪnd ɪz lˈaɪk ɐ klˈoʊzd bˈʊk dʒˈʌst ɐ blˈɑːk ʌv wˈʊd

senses.definition: used to imply that when someone is not open to new ideas or experiences, they are limiting their own personal and intellectual growth

word_list.word: when a twig grows hard, it (is|becomes) difficult to twist (it|)
word_list.pronunciation: wˌɛn ɐ twˈɪɡ ɡɹˈoʊz hˈɑːɹd ɪt ɪz bɪkˌʌmz dˈɪfɪkəlt tə twˈɪst ɪt

senses.definition: used to imply that it is easier to influence and shape someone's character and behavior when they are young, as it is difficult to change once they become set in their ways

word_list.word: you cannot shift an old tree without it dying
word_list.phonetic_text: ju kæn.nɑt ʃɪft æn oʊld tri: wɪð.aʊt ʌɪti: daɪɪng
word_list.phonetic_am_text: yoo kān.naat shift ān ooold tri vidh.aoot aiti daiing
word_list.pronunciation: juː kænˈɑːt ʃˈɪft ɐn ˈoʊld tɹˈiː wɪðˌaʊt ɪt dˈaɪɪŋ

senses.definition: used to highlight the challenges and risks associated with attempting to disrupt or change long-standing or deeply rooted aspects of a person or system

word_list.word: a new broom sweeps clean, but the old brush knows all the corners
word_list.pronunciation: ɐ nˈuː bɹˈuːm swˈiːps klˈiːn bˌʌt ðɪ ˈoʊld bɹˈʌʃ nˈoʊz ˈɔːl ðə kˈɔːɹnɚz

senses.definition: used to suggest that while the new may bring efficiency, the old has valuable experience and knowledge, and so combining both can lead to optimal outcomes
